Patch Set 1: Code-Review-2

See of chain of commits can be clubbed together in single review !

Patch Set 1: Code-Review+1

Ok again... I could join an ONAP Technical meeting if it helps.

Sorry Dan your proposal is breaking the best practices.
The 2 changes are cherry picked and then must be merged without any change if possible.

Please read https://docs.openstack.org/project-team-guide/stable-branches.html
I would have got -1 in OpenStack if I applied your proposal (I don't think someone would dare voting -2 in OpenStack for this kind of patches)

Patch Set 1:

> Patch Set 1: Code-Review+1
> 
> Ok again... I could join an ONAP Technical meeting if it helps.
> 
> Sorry Dan your proposal is breaking the best practices.
> The 2 changes are cherry picked and then must be merged without any change if possible.
> 
> Please read https://docs.openstack.org/project-team-guide/stable-branches.html
> I would have got -1 in OpenStack if I applied your proposal (I don't think someone would dare voting -2 in OpenStack for this kind of patches)

Hello Cedric,
Thanks for your contribution. 

Where were these cherry picked from? Looks like the patch has been altered already as it says "jakarta" which would not belong in any other branch in this repository. Per the guidelines you attach, a cherry-picked patch should also keep the original change-id, but these are unique? I see that you made the same change several other projects but that is not cherry-picking.  

Do these patches work independently or you need all 3 to achieve the purpose of your task? I can see that the .gitreview change makes sense on its own, but the other two definitely look like they belong together. These are very small changes that just modify references to a branch, what is the advantage of having 2 commits for this rather than 1?

Patch Set 1:

> Patch Set 1:
> 
> > Patch Set 1: Code-Review+1
> > 
> > Ok again... I could join an ONAP Technical meeting if it helps.
> > 
> > Sorry Dan your proposal is breaking the best practices.
> > The 2 changes are cherry picked and then must be merged without any change if possible.
> > 
> > Please read https://docs.openstack.org/project-team-guide/stable-branches.html
> > I would have got -1 in OpenStack if I applied your proposal (I don't think someone would dare voting -2 in OpenStack for this kind of patches)
> 
> Hello Cedric,
> Thanks for your contribution. 
> 
> Where were these cherry picked from? Looks like the patch has been altered already as it says "jakarta" which would not belong in any other branch in this repository. Per the guidelines you attach, a cherry-picked patch should also keep the original change-id, but these are unique? I see that you made the same change several other projects but that is not cherry-picking.  
> 
> Do these patches work independently or you need all 3 to achieve the purpose of your task? I can see that the .gitreview change makes sense on its own, but the other two definitely look like they belong together. These are very small changes that just modify references to a branch, what is the advantage of having 2 commits for this rather than 1?

Hello Jozsef

Change-Id uniqueness is per branch.
It is (among other things) used by Gerrit to detect changes that were backported(=cherry-picked) to another branch.
This information is usually displayed on the to right corner of the web interface
The cherry-pick must not alter the change-Id in that case, otherwise Gerrit won't be able to detect the backport. 
The .git-review change is effectively independent. Its only purpose is to indicate to git-review the branch were to push the commit. Otherwise, it will use the master branch by default.
If you are new to Gerrit, I  wrote a guide for newcomers at https://docs.opendaylight.org/en/stable-phosphorus/contributor-guides/newcomers-guide.html

This change manly affects the content built for the documentation by making references point to the right branch.
The previous one is about fixing dependencies used to generate the documentation with tox locally or in the CI. It also adds concrete dependencies mainly for the CI.
It does not affect directly the content of the documentation.

From that standpoint they address 2 very different issues. In another branch, one of them might not need to be fixed.
Having 2 different commits  allow to backport only one of them in that case for example.
Other advantages of the approach are explained in Openstack wiki at 
https://wiki.openstack.org/wiki/GitCommitMessages
But in nutshell, Having atomic commits is completely part of the Gerrit and KISS philosophy and best practices.

Hope this helps
Guillaume

These are very small changes that just modify references to a branch, what is the advantage of having 2 commits for this rather than 1?

Change has been successfully merged by Jozsef Csongvai
